questions.text: How are users authenticated to access corporate resources?
questions.rationale: Authentication strength is the foundation of Zero Trust — every access request must be verified.

options.label: Username and password only
options.description: Static credentials with no additional factors

options.label: MFA for some applications or user groups
options.description: Partial MFA adoption — high-risk apps or admins only

options.label: MFA enforced for all users and all applications
Continuous, risk-adaptive authentication
options.description: Step-up auth triggered by risk signals; passwordless preferred

questions.text: How is privileged access (admin accounts) managed?
questions.rationale: Privileged accounts are the highest-value targets — ZT requires they are tightly scoped and monitored.

options.label: Shared admin accounts or standing privileges
Named admin accounts with basic separation of duties
PAM solution with session recording and approval workflows
options.description: Privileged Access Management controls in place

options.label: Just-in-Time (JIT) privileges with zero standing access
options.description: Ephemeral admin rights, fully audited, auto-expired

questions.text: How are access rights reviewed and recertified?
questions.rationale: Stale permissions accumulate over time — regular recertification enforces least privilege.

options.label: Rarely or never reviewed
options.description: Access is assigned and largely unchanged

options.label: Annual manual reviews
options.description: Periodic reviews driven by compliance deadlines

options.label: Quarterly automated access reviews with manager certification
Continuous automated access reviews with ML-based anomaly detection

questions.text: Is Single Sign-On (SSO) implemented across applications?
questions.rationale: SSO centralizes identity control and enables consistent policy enforcement across all apps.

options.label: No SSO — users manage separate credentials per application
SSO for some internal applications
SSO for all internal and most SaaS applications
Universal SSO with continuous session risk evaluation
options.description: All apps federated; session trust dynamically evaluated

questions.text: How is non-person entity (service account, API, workload) identity managed?
questions.rationale: Machine identities are frequently overlooked and represent a major ZT gap.

options.label: Shared credentials or hardcoded secrets
options.description: No lifecycle management for machine identities

options.label: Named service accounts with static credentials
Secrets manager with rotation policies
options.description: Centralized vault with automated credential rotation

options.label: Workload identity federation with short-lived tokens (OIDC/SPIFFE)

questions.text: Is User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) deployed?
questions.rationale: UEBA detects insider threats and compromised accounts by baselining normal behavior.

options.label: No behavioral monitoring
options.description: No visibility into user activity patterns

options.label: Basic audit logging with manual review
UEBA integrated with SIEM — alerts on anomalies
UEBA with automated adaptive response (block, step-up auth)
options.description: Real-time risk-based response to identity anomalies

questions.text: Is identity governance (IGA) implemented for joiner-mover-leaver processes?
questions.rationale: Lifecycle gaps create orphaned accounts — a leading cause of unauthorized access.

options.label: Manual, inconsistent HR-to-IT processes
options.description: Delayed or missed de-provisioning is common

options.label: Basic automation for joiners; leavers handled manually
IGA platform with automated provisioning and de-provisioning
options.description: Full lifecycle managed via IGA workflows

options.label: IGA with real-time HR feed integration and zero-day de-provisioning
options.description: Instant access termination on employee departure

questions.text: How is device health and compliance verified before granting access?
questions.rationale: ZT requires that every device proves its health posture — not just its identity.

options.label: No device health checks — any device can access resources
Basic MDM enrollment required for corporate devices
options.description: Enrollment checked but not real-time posture

options.label: Device compliance policy enforced at access time via MDM/EDR integration
Continuous device attestation with hardware-level trust (TPM/Secure Enclave)

questions.text: Is a complete and continuously updated device inventory maintained?
questions.rationale: You cannot protect what you cannot see — full asset visibility is a ZT prerequisite.

options.label: No formal inventory — devices tracked informally
Periodic manual inventory or basic MDM asset list
options.description: Inventory exists but may be stale

options.label: Automated discovery with near-real-time inventory updates
Continuous real-time inventory with automatic policy enforcement for new devices

questions.text: How is Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) deployed?
questions.rationale: EDR provides the telemetry needed to evaluate device trust in a ZT model.

options.label: Traditional antivirus only
EDR deployed on most managed endpoints
options.description: Partial coverage — some gaps in visibility

options.label: EDR deployed on all managed endpoints with centralized management
EDR with AI-driven threat hunting and ZT policy integration
options.description: EDR posture signals feed directly into access decisions

questions.text: How are unmanaged and BYOD devices handled?
questions.rationale: Unmanaged devices are the highest-risk access vector — ZT requires explicit policy for these.

options.label: No differentiation — same access as managed devices
options.description: No controls on unmanaged device access

options.label: BYOD access restricted to email and basic productivity via MDM profile
BYOD isolated to browser-based access or VDI — no corporate data on device
BYOD assessed in real-time; access scoped dynamically based on posture

questions.text: Is patch and vulnerability management automated for endpoints?
questions.rationale: Unpatched endpoints are exploitable — ZT requires proof of patch currency as part of device trust.

options.label: Manual patching — infrequent and inconsistent
Automated patching for OS; applications patched manually
Automated patching for OS and applications with compliance reporting
Continuous vulnerability assessment with automated remediation and access gating for non-compliant devices
options.description: Patch currency enforced at access time

questions.text: Is Mobile Device Management (MDM) / Unified Endpoint Management (UEM) deployed?
questions.rationale: MDM/UEM enables configuration enforcement and remote wipe — essential for device trust.

options.label: No MDM — endpoints are unmanaged
MDM for mobile devices only
UEM covering all device types (mobile, laptop, desktop)
UEM with real-time compliance enforcement and ZT policy integration

questions.text: Are device certificates or hardware keys used for device authentication?
questions.rationale: Device certificates provide cryptographic proof of device identity — stronger than IP or hostname.

options.label: No device authentication — devices identified by IP or hostname only
PKI certificates deployed on some device types
PKI certificates deployed on all managed devices with automated renewal
Hardware-backed certificates (TPM) with automated certificate management

questions.text: Is network micro-segmentation implemented?
questions.rationale: Flat networks allow lateral movement — micro-segmentation is a core ZT network control.

options.label: Flat network — minimal segmentation beyond DMZ
options.description: Lateral movement unconstrained once inside perimeter

options.label: VLAN-based segmentation for broad zones (prod, dev, corp)
Micro-segmentation with firewall rules per application or workload
Software-defined micro-segmentation with identity-aware policies enforced at the workload level
options.description: ZT network fabric — every workload is a segment

questions.text: How is east-west (internal) traffic inspected?
questions.rationale: Most breaches involve lateral movement — internal traffic inspection is critical for ZT.

options.label: No inspection of internal traffic
options.description: Implicit trust within the network perimeter

options.label: Basic network monitoring (NetFlow/IPFIX) on internal traffic
IDS/IPS on internal segments with alerting
Full SSL/TLS inspection with behavioral analytics on all internal flows
options.description: No implicit trust on any network segment

questions.text: Is Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) or Software-Defined Perimeter (SDP) deployed for remote access?
questions.rationale: VPNs grant broad network access — ZTNA replaces VPN with application-level access.

options.label: Traditional VPN — full network access once authenticated
VPN with split-tunneling or application-level controls
options.description: Partial access restriction via VPN policy

options.label: ZTNA deployed for remote access — users access specific apps not the network
ZTNA for all remote access with continuous posture evaluation per session
options.description: Full ZTNA with real-time trust evaluation

questions.text: How is encrypted traffic (TLS) inspected on the network?
questions.rationale: Encrypted traffic can hide threats — ZT requires visibility even into encrypted flows.

options.label: No TLS inspection — encrypted traffic is untrusted but uninspected
options.description: Threats can hide in encrypted channels

options.label: TLS inspection at the perimeter firewall/proxy only
TLS inspection at multiple points (perimeter, internal, cloud egress)
Comprehensive TLS inspection with certificate pinning enforcement and anomaly detection

questions.text: Is DNS security implemented?
questions.rationale: DNS is a primary C2 channel — DNS security prevents exfiltration and malware callbacks.

options.label: No DNS filtering or security controls
Basic DNS filtering via on-prem resolver (block known-bad domains)
Cloud-delivered DNS security (filtering, DoH enforcement, DNSSEC validation)
DNS analytics integrated with SIEM; anomalous DNS used as threat signal in ZT policy
options.description: DNS as a ZT telemetry source

questions.text: Is Software-Defined WAN (SD-WAN) or cloud-delivered network security (SSE/SASE) in use?
questions.rationale: SSE/SASE converges network and security, enabling consistent ZT policy across all locations.

options.label: Traditional MPLS/WAN with on-prem security appliances
SD-WAN deployed — traffic optimization but security still appliance-based
SSE/SASE with cloud-delivered SWG, CASB, and ZTNA
Full SASE with identity-aware routing, continuous posture checks, and unified policy across all edges

questions.text: How are internal applications published and accessed?
questions.rationale: How applications are exposed determines the attack surface — ZT requires app-level access, not network access.

options.label: Direct network access — apps accessible to anyone on the corporate network
VPN required for internal app access — broad network tunnel granted
App proxy or ZTNA — users access specific apps without network access
All apps published via ZTNA/SDP with per-session trust evaluation and least-privilege access

questions.text: Is API security and management centralized?
questions.rationale: APIs are the modern attack surface — undocumented and unmanaged APIs are a major ZT gap.

options.label: No API gateway — APIs called directly; no centralized visibility
API gateway for some APIs — others are undocumented or unmanaged
All APIs registered in an API management platform with authentication and rate limiting
API discovery, runtime protection, and behavioral analytics on all API traffic

questions.text: Is workload identity implemented for cloud and containerized environments?
questions.rationale: In cloud-native environments, workloads must authenticate to each other — not just humans to systems.

options.label: Workloads use static credentials or IAM keys stored in code/config
Managed identities or instance profiles used for some cloud resources
Managed identities / IRSA / Workload Identity used across all cloud workloads
SPIFFE/SPIRE or equivalent for cross-environment workload identity with short-lived mTLS certificates

questions.text: Is application security testing integrated into the CI/CD pipeline?
questions.rationale: ZT extends to the software supply chain — apps must be verified secure before deployment.

options.label: No automated security testing in the pipeline
options.description: Security tested manually or not at all

options.label: SAST scanning integrated; results reviewed periodically
SAST, DAST, and SCA all integrated with pipeline gates on critical findings
Full DevSecOps: SAST, DAST, SCA, IaC scanning, container scanning, and SBOMs with policy enforcement
options.description: Security as code — ZT for the supply chain

questions.text: Is SaaS application usage discovered and governed?
questions.rationale: Shadow SaaS creates data exposure and identity sprawl — CASB visibility is a ZT requirement.

options.label: No SaaS discovery — unknown SaaS apps are in use
CASB in monitoring mode — SaaS discovered but not enforced
CASB enforcing policy — approved SaaS list, DLP controls on SaaS data
Inline CASB with real-time SaaS risk scoring and adaptive access control
options.description: Dynamic SaaS trust based on risk

questions.text: Is secrets management centralized for application credentials?
questions.rationale: Hardcoded secrets are a top breach vector — ZT requires centralized, audited secrets lifecycle.

options.label: Secrets stored in code, config files, or shared spreadsheets
Secrets stored in environment variables or basic vaults with manual rotation
Centralized secrets vault with automated rotation and audit logging
options.description: Secrets managed with full lifecycle control

options.label: Dynamic secrets with zero long-lived credentials; secret scanning in pipelines
options.description: No persistent secrets anywhere in the stack

questions.text: Is application behavior monitored for runtime anomalies?
questions.rationale: ZT assumes breach — runtime application protection detects attacks that bypass perimeter defenses.

options.label: No runtime monitoring — issues detected reactively via user reports
Basic APM for availability and performance — no security monitoring
Application logging integrated with SIEM; alerts on error rate spikes and auth failures
RASP or behavioral analytics detecting zero-day attacks in real-time with automated response

questions.text: Is data classified and labeled across the organization?
questions.rationale: ZT data controls depend on knowing what data you have and how sensitive it is.

options.label: No formal data classification — all data treated equally
options.description: Cannot apply differentiated controls without classification

options.label: Classification policy exists but labeling is manual and inconsistent
Automated classification and labeling for structured and unstructured data
AI-driven classification across all data stores with real-time discovery of new sensitive data

questions.text: Is Data Loss Prevention (DLP) implemented?
questions.rationale: DLP enforces data handling policies — preventing unauthorized exfiltration of sensitive data.

options.label: No DLP controls
options.description: Data can leave the organization unconstrained

options.label: DLP on email only
options.description: Limited DLP coverage — endpoints and cloud are unprotected

options.label: DLP covering email, endpoints, and cloud storage
options.description: Broad DLP coverage across major channels

options.label: Unified DLP policy across all channels (email, endpoint, cloud, web) with adaptive enforcement
options.description: Comprehensive data protection with context-aware enforcement

questions.text: Is data encrypted at rest and in transit for all sensitive assets?
questions.rationale: Encryption ensures that even if data is accessed, it cannot be read without authorization.

options.label: Encryption inconsistently applied — many sensitive data stores are unencrypted
Encryption at rest for databases; TLS for data in transit
Encryption at rest and in transit for all sensitive data, including backups and archives
Customer-managed keys (CMEK/BYOK) with HSM, key rotation policies, and envelope encryption

questions.text: How is data access governed — who can access what data and why?
questions.rationale: ZT requires attribute-based or role-based access to data with audit trails on every access.

options.label: Minimal access controls — most data accessible to anyone in the organization
Role-based access control (RBAC) for primary data systems
Attribute-based access control (ABAC) with data classification driving access policy
Automated entitlement management with continuous access graph optimization and just-in-time data access

questions.text: Is all data access logged and auditable?
questions.rationale: Forensic capability requires knowing who accessed what data, when, and from where.

options.label: Minimal or no data access logging
Logging for some sensitive data stores
Comprehensive data access logging across all sensitive systems, retained for 12+ months
Real-time data access monitoring with anomaly alerts and automated incident triggering

questions.text: Is Cloud Data Security Posture Management (DSPM) or database activity monitoring deployed?
questions.rationale: Cloud data stores often contain unknown sensitive data — DSPM discovers and protects them.

options.label: No visibility into cloud data store contents or access patterns
options.description: Unknown sensitive data exposure in cloud

options.label: Manual audits of cloud storage — periodic reviews of bucket/blob permissions
DSPM discovering sensitive data in cloud stores and flagging misconfigurations
DSPM with real-time misconfiguration remediation and data access behavioral analytics

questions.text: Is a centralized SIEM/SOAR platform deployed with coverage across all pillars?
questions.rationale: ZT requires comprehensive telemetry and automated response — SIEM/SOAR is the control plane for security operations.

options.label: No SIEM — logs collected ad-hoc; no centralized visibility
SIEM deployed but with limited source coverage — key pillars have gaps
SIEM with coverage across identity, endpoint, network, and application logs; basic SOAR playbooks
SIEM/SOAR with full pillar coverage, threat intelligence enrichment, and automated ZT policy enforcement on detections

questions.text: Is continuous compliance monitoring and automated reporting implemented?
questions.rationale: ZT requires provable compliance — continuous monitoring replaces point-in-time audits.

options.label: Compliance assessed annually via manual audit
Automated compliance scans run periodically (monthly/quarterly)
options.description: More frequent visibility but still lagging

options.label: Continuous compliance monitoring with dashboard and automated evidence collection
Policy-as-code with automated remediation and continuous compliance attestation across all ZT pillars

questions.text: What is the organization's Security Operations Center (SOC) operating model?
questions.rationale: ZT requires continuous monitoring — the SOC model determines whether detections are acted on in real time or discovered hours later. Coverage gaps directly increase dwell time.

options.label: No formal SOC — security alerts reviewed ad-hoc by IT staff
options.description: No dedicated detection and response capability

options.label: Business-hours SOC with on-call coverage — alerts may go unactioned overnight
24/7 in-house or MDR/MSSP SOC with defined escalation playbooks
24/7 SOC with ZT-integrated playbooks — automated containment and policy enforcement on confirmed detections
options.description: Closed-loop detection and automated ZT response

questions.text: Are defined SLAs in place for mean time to detect (MTTD) and mean time to respond (MTTR) to security incidents?
questions.rationale: ZT requires measurable security operations. Without SLAs, organizations cannot quantify or improve detection and response capability over time.

options.label: No defined SLAs — incident response is fully ad-hoc
Informal MTTD/MTTR targets exist but are not formally tracked or reported
Formal MTTD/MTTR SLAs defined, measured, and reported to security leadership
SLAs continuously optimized through automated playbooks, tabletop exercises, and post-incident reviews

questions.text: Is threat intelligence integrated into detection and response workflows?
questions.rationale: Operationalized threat intelligence transforms raw indicators into actionable detections, reducing dwell time for known threat actor TTPs relevant to the organization's sector.

options.label: No threat intelligence feeds — detection relies on default vendor signatures only
Threat intelligence feeds subscribed to but manually reviewed — not automated into detection rules
Threat intelligence automatically ingested into SIEM with IOC matching and TTP-based detection rules
Threat intelligence platform (TIP) with automated rule generation, proactive threat hunting, and sector-specific feeds
